coredata.dc:title: Xylan derived carbon dots composite with PCL/PLA for construction biomass nanofiber membrane used as fluorescence sensor for detection Cu2+ in real samples
coredata.dc:creator: Cui, Xiaoci
Zhang, Yingying
Chen, Zhiyuan
Xiao, Huining
Xiong, Ranhua
Huang, Chaobo
coredata.dc:description: Water and soil pollution caused by Cu2+ is not conducive to sustainable development of environment and could cause damage to environment and even human body. Currently, fluorescent sensor solutions analysis method has been used for Cu2+ detection, but they also suffer from drawbacks including easy leakage, difficult storage, and inaccurate. Herein, a green solid-state biomass fluorescence platform (NBU-CDs) consisting of xylan-derived carbon dots (U-CDs) and polylactic acid/polycaprolactone (PLA/PCL) was designed by using in situ electrospinning technology. The prepared NBU-CDs fluorescence platform showed good fluorescence effect and can be served as fluorescence sensor for detecting Cu2+ with high sensitively, selectively and low detection limit (LOD = 0.83 μM). The practical applications of NBU-CDs exhibited high specificity for Cu2+ detection in zebrafish, water samples (school lake, Xuanwu Lake and Yangtze River) with high recovery rates of 97 %–104 % and soil (pond soil, grassland soil and bamboo soil) samples, respectively. The developed fluorescence platform was utilized to predict water and soil safety by monitoring Cu2+ concentration and provides a new strategy for Cu2+ detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Xylan derived carbon dots
Fluorescence nanofiber membrane
Cu2+ detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of fluorescence nanofibers membrane NBU-CDs
Fluorescence property of NBU-CDs for detection Cu2+
Practical application of NBU-CDs for detection Cu2+

National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We also thank the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22275094 , 22275093 ) and Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province ( KYCX23_1159, SJCX23_0328 ) for giving us supporting.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation and schematic diagram of xylan derived CDs-based biomass nanofiber membranes serves as solid state fluorescence sensor for Cu2+ detection.

(A) Preparation and schematic diagram of xylan derived biomass CDs (U-CDs). (B) TEM of U-CDs. (C) Particle size distribution of U-CDs. (D) HRTEM of U-CDs. (E) Ultraviolet-visible absorption spectrum of U-CDs. Inset: Images of U-CDs under sunlight (left) and Uv-vis light (right). (F) Excitation characteristic of U-CDs. (G) FTIR spectrum of U-CDs. (H) High-resolution XPS spectra of C1s. (I) High -resolution XPS spectra of N1s. (J) High -resolution XPS spectra of O1s.

(A) Preparation of biomass fluorescent nanofiber membrane and its schematic diagram for Cu2+ detection. (B) SEM image of NBU-CDs. (C) Size distribution of NBU-CDs. (D) EDS analysis of NBU-CDs. (E) SEM image of NBU-CDs + Cu2+. (F) Size distribution of NBU-CDs + Cu2+. (G) EDS analysis of NBU-CDs + Cu2+.
Fluorescence confocal microscopy images of NBU-CDs and NBU-CDs with Cu2+ under bright and green channel, respectively.

(A) Selectivity and anti-interference ability of NBU-CDs and NBU-CDs + Cu2+ with various ions including (20 μM, K+, Ca2+, Na+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Hg2+, Fe2+, Ni+, Cu2+, Al3+, Fe3+, Cr3+, Ni2+, F−, CN−, ACO−, HPO4 2−, NO2 −, SCN− and HS−) (λ ex = 350 nm, silt = 3.5 nm), respectively. (B) Binding mechanism of NBU-CDs + Cu2+. (C) Fluorescence titration curve of NBU-CDs with different concentrations of Cu2+ (0–20 μM). (D) Time stability and fluorescence stability of NBU-CDs and NBU-CDs + Cu2+ (λ ex = 350 nm, silt = 3.5 nm).

(A) Schematic diagram of using NBU-CDs to detect external Cu2+ in soil samples. (B) Fluorescence color change of soil extract solution mixed with NBU-CDs for Cu2+ detection. (C) Fluorescence intensity change of soil extract solution mixed with NBU-CDs for Cu2+ detection.

(A) Growth of mung beans after co-cultivation with different concentrations of U-CDs (10 μM and 50 μM). (B) Germination rate of mung beans after co-cultivation with different concentrations of U-CDs (10 μM and 50 μM). (C) Fluorescence images of zebrafish embryo and adult zebrafish after co-cultured with NBU-CDs and NBU-CDs + Cu2+ under green channel. (D) Normalized fluorescence intensity of zebrafish embryo and adult zebrafish after co-cultured with NBU-CDs and NBU-CDs + Cu2+ under green channel. (E) Biocompatibility of NBU-CDs cultured with zebrafish for 15 days. (F) Survival rate of NBU-CDs cultured with zebrafish for 15 days.
Comparison of the detection performance of fluorescence sensor.
Detection of Cu2+ in real water samples.

head.ce:title: Xylan derived carbon dots composite with PCL/PLA for construction biomass nanofiber membrane used as fluorescence sensor for detection Cu2+ in real samples

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Joint Laboratory of Advanced Biomedical Materials (NFU-UGent), Jiangsu Co-Innovation Center of Efficient Processing and Utilization of Forest Resources, College of Chemical Engineering, Nanjing Forestry University (NFU), Nanjing 210037, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Joint Laboratory of Advanced Biomedical Materials (NFU-UGent)
Jiangsu Co-Innovation Center of Efficient Processing and Utilization of Forest Resources

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Joint Laboratory of Advanced Biomedical Materials (NFU-UGent), Jiangsu Co-Innovation Center of Efficient Processing and Utilization of Forest Resources, College of Chemical Engineering, Nanjing Forestry University (NFU), Nanjing 210037, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, University of New Brunswick, Fredericton, New Brunswick E3B5A3, Canada
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering, University of New Brunswick, Fredericton, New Brunswick, E3B5A3, Canada

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors (Xiaoci Cui and Yingying Zhang) contributed equally to this manuscript.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Water and soil pollution caused by Cu2+ is not conducive to sustainable development of environment and could cause damage to environment and even human body. Currently, fluorescent sensor solutions analysis method has been used for Cu2+ detection, but they also suffer from drawbacks including easy leakage, difficult storage, and inaccurate. Herein, a green solid-state biomass fluorescence platform (NBU-CDs) consisting of xylan-derived carbon dots (U-CDs) and polylactic acid/polycaprolactone (PLA/PCL) was designed by using in situ electrospinning technology. The prepared NBU-CDs fluorescence platform showed good fluorescence effect and can be served as fluorescence sensor for detecting Cu2+ with high sensitively, selectively and low detection limit (LOD = 0.83 μM). The practical applications of NBU-CDs exhibited high specificity for Cu2+ detection in zebrafish, water samples (school lake, Xuanwu Lake and Yangtze River) with high recovery rates of 97 %–104 % and soil (pond soil, grassland soil and bamboo soil) samples, respectively. The developed fluorescence platform was utilized to predict water and soil safety by monitoring Cu2+ concentration and provides a new strategy for Cu2+ detection.

ce:section.ce:para: At present, copper (Cu) has a wide range of applications in renewable energy, construction, electrical equipment, and industrial machinery, and is also the third most abundant transition metal element in the human body after iron and zinc [1–4]. Appropriate amount of Cu has a promoting effect on the growth and development of animals and plants. When Cu content in the soil exceeds the maximum allowable value, Cu pollution will occur and further posing a threat to plants, animals and microorganisms, which damaging the soil ecology [5–7]. Further, Cu in soil could also enter the human body through food, and when it is excessive in the body, it poses a threat to human health [8–11]. Cu2+ is essential in the electron transfer process of many biological reactions. Lacking of Cu2+ in the body can affect enzyme activity, cause gastrointestinal discomfort, abnormal red blood cell formation, and inhibit cell metabolism, leading to various neurological problems [12–15]. Besides, excessive Cu2+ in the body can lead to gastrointestinal disorders and liver and kidney damage, causing adverse effects on the body [16,17]. For the safe ingestion of Cu2+ in drinking water, World Health Organization (WHO) and Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) have set the current guideline values at 2 mg/L (∼30 μM) and 1.3 mg/L (∼20 μM), respectively. Therefore, it's urgently to develop a sensitively method for Cu2+ detection.

Conventional analytical methods for Cu2+ determination mainly include atomic absorption spectroscopy, inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry and inductively coupled plasma emission spectrometry [18–20], which exhibiting good detection limits and working ranges. However, they also suffer from some drawbacks such as high detection costs, require professional personnel to operate, cannot be tested on site, and require fine purification of samples before use and time-consuming sample preparation steps [21–23]. Therefore, it is urgent to establish a selective, convenient, fast and cost-effective method for detection of Cu2+.

Fluorescent analysis method usually depicts good advantages of simplicity, fast response, high selectivity and sensitivity and non-invasiveness, which have attracted much attention in recent years. Especially, fluorescence carbon dots (CDs) have been widely used as fluorescence sensor, bioimaging, biosensors, light-emitting diode materials and visible light active photocatalysts [24–33]. Importantly, fluorescence CDs are widely used in detection of heavy metal ions due to their excellent luminescence and fluorescence properties [34]. Shuang et al., designed a red-emitting nitrogen-doped CDs (N-CDs) solution for fluorescence and colorimetric dual-mode detection of Cu2+ with a detection limit (LOD) of 45.87 μM [16]. Zhou et al., prepared a colorimetric h-CDs solution used as fluorescent probe to detect Cu2+ with a detection limit of 18.0 μM [12]. Navarro et al., developed a ZnSe/ZnS quantum-dots (QDs) solution for Cu2+ sensing by fluorescence quenching method, showing good sensitivity with LOD of 0.17 μM [35]. It worth noting that biomass CDs have attracted widespread attention in recent years as a promising carbon material due to their unique advantages such as wide source, renewable, good bio-compatibility, low toxicity, and photo-stability. Wang et al., developed a new type of green carbon quantum dots (ILB-CQDs) solution using grape skin as carbon source for the selective detection of Fe3+ and Pd2+ with a detection limit of 3.2 nmol/L for Fe3+ and 0.36 μmol/L for Pd2+ in actual water [36]. Kim et al., synthesized a CDs solution using coffee waste via a simple hydrothermal treatment for dopamine (DA) detection with a low detection limit of 4.25 nM [37]. These fluorescence CDs solutions have high sensitivity and selectivity towards Cu2+ and other heavy metal ions, but due to the solution state, they usually suffering the drawbacks of difficult to store and carry, further limiting their application in fluorescence detection.

Solid-state nanofiber membrane prepared by electrospinning technology has the advantages of large specific surface area, low porosity and excellent biocompatibility, portability, high stability, real-time detection, easy fabrication and integration with flexible device [38–41]. In addition, electrospinning nanofibers membrane with a large surface area ratio, high porosity, and network-like structures can used as matrix and enable more fluorescent sensors and other recognition molecules to selectively combine with the object to be measured through non covalent bond or covalent bond bonding, and induce charge transfer or spatial structure change of recognition molecules [42,43]. Macroscopic performance of nanofibers membrane is absorption spectrum and color change of recognition molecules, which can effectively improve the sensitivity of colorimetric and spectral sensors [32,44–46]. For the fluorescence sensing platform based on nanofiber membrane, fluorescent materials such as small organic molecules, CDs and QDs are generally doped into the spinning solution of the polymer precursor for detection metal ions, pH, bimolecular and other analytes with ‘turn-on’ or ‘turn-off’ response [41] and visual effect. Hence, using nanofiber membrane as fluorescence sensor is a promising method for detection.

Herein, a strategy of mixing CDs fluorescent solution and electrospinning precursor solution to prepare biomass fluorescent nanofiber membrane (NBU-CDs) via electrospinning technology was presented. And its ability as a solid-state fluorescent sensor to detect heavy metal Cu2+ was also explored, as shown in Fig. 1 . In detail, biomass derived N-doped CDs (U-CDs) were prepared by using xylan as carbon source, branched polyethyleneimine (B-PEI) and urea as dopants. Then, electrospinning precursor containing U-CDs was prepared in situ by electrospinning technology. According to the microscopic images of SEM analysis, fluorescence confocal microscopy and fluorescence emission spectrum, the NBU-CDs has high selectivity, sensitivity and obvious fluorescence quenching response behavior to Cu2+. Importantly, NBU-CDs can detect Cu2+ in different soil types and exhibit obvious fluorescence quenching behavior in suit and real time, indicating that it can be used as a fluorescence sensor to detect Cu2+ in soil. Besides, NBU-CDs also has good detection ability, fluorescence imaging and high bio-compatibility for exogenous Cu2+ in zebrafish and its embryos. This study provides a green synthesis strategy of solid-state biomass fluorescence platform. Additionally, it lays the foundation for further advancements in real-time and in-situ detecting of Cu2+ in real water environment.

Deionized water used in the experiment was highly purified before use. Xylan (from birch, 95 %), sodium phytate (99 %), polyethyleneimine-branched (B-PEI, average Mw = 800 by LS, average Mn = 600 by GPC), Urea (99 %) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Reagent Co., LTD. Polycaprolactone (PCL, average Mn = 45,000), polylactic acid (PLA, Mw ∼60,000), hexafluoroisopropanol (HFIP, 99.5 %) were all purchased from Macklin Co., Ltd. Soil samples were obtained from bamboo forests, small rivers, and ponds from school, respectively. Regenerated cellulose membrane tubing (American, Mw 500 Da) used in this experiment were all purchased from Macklin Co., Ltd. Zebrafish embryos were purchased from Shanghai Biotechnology Co., Ltd. Portable UV–vis lamp (365 nm) was purchased from local shop.

Xylan was purified according a previous publication [47]. NaOH solution (20 mL, 2 %) was added into a beaker that containing xylan (5 g), then anhydrous ethanol (35 mL, 99 %) was added into above solution and magnetic stirred for 4 h. After xylan was completely precipitated and white succulent was precipitated, the mixed solution was left to stand. Then, xylan was filtered through 0.45 μm filter membranes to obtain xylan powder with high purity.

Purified xylan (0.1 g) and B-PEI (0.3 g) were added into deionized water (20 mL) under continuous stirring for 1 h. Homogeneous mixture was transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave (50 mL) and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature, the mixture was collected and separated by dialyzed in a regenerated cellulose membrane tubing (retained molecular weight: 500 Da) with deionized water for 48 h. Then solution was collected, namely U-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of fluorescence nanofibers membrane NBU-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Solid state nanofibers fluorescence membrane NBU-CDs were prepared by using in-situ electrospinning technology. Electrospinning precursor solution mixtures including PLA (12 wt%) and PCL (12 wt%) were obtained by dissolving PLA (1.2 g) and PCL (1.2 g) in HFIP solution (10 mL) with overnight stirring [38]. Then U-CDs solution (10 mg/mL) was added into above mixtures and stirred for 6 h. Above spinning precursors were added into a syringe (5 mL) and form fibers under the action of high voltage, and finally deposited on the collector with the 1.3 mL/h feed rate of the syringe pump. Besides, the distance between the needle tip and fly wheel collector (300 r/min) was 18 cm. A voltage of 18 kV was applied using a high voltage station. The control nanofiber membranes (PCL/PLA) were prepared by the same method as above, except that U-CDs was removed.

To study the selectivity of NBU-CDs towards other substances including metal ions and cations, appropriate concentrations of various substances (Control, K+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Ag+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Cr3+, Al3+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Cu2+, F−, Cl−, CN−, HPO4 2−, ClO−, NO2−, SCN− and HS−) were diluted in ultra-pure water (10 mL). The fluorescence intensity was recorded in the wavelength range of 350 nm–1000 nm with an excitation wavelength of 350 nm, silt: 3 nm.

Quinine sulfate (QY = 55 % in 0.1 mol L−1 H2SO4) was used as the reference. The fluorescence quantum yield is 54 % at 360 nm excitation wavelength. The optical absorbance of sample aqueous solution and reference substance was measured. In order to reduce the reabsorption effect of fluorescent substances as much as possible, the concentrations of reference solution and sample solution were adjusted to make their absorbance values less than 0.05. Then, the fluorescence spectra of quinine sulfate and sample aqueous solution with known absorbance were tested. The QY of the sample was then calculated according to the following equation [48]:
(2-1)
φ =
φ 0
×
A 0
I 0
×
I A
×
n 2
n 0 2
where φ , A, I, and n represent the QY, optical density, integral emission intensity, and refractive index (water = 1.33, ethanol = 1.36) of the sample, respectively, and
φ 0
, A 0, I 0, and n 0 represent the QY, optical density, integral emission intensity, and refractive index of quinine sulfate.

As the main component of hemicellulose, xylan accounts for about 15 %–35 % of the dry weight of plant cells. It is a kind of polypentasaccharide from a wide range of sources [49]. At the same time, xylan has good water solubility, low carbonization temperature and high proportion of carbon atoms, which is an ideal carbon source for the synthesis of CDs [50]. Xylan are composed of three elements, C, H and O, and the content of C is more than 40 %, which can ensure the formation of the main carbon skeleton structure of CDs in the nucleation process. On the other hand, the oxygen-containing groups of xylan also create conditions for formation of fluorescence emission potential well and heteroatomic doping sites of CDs. Therefore, xylan as cheap renewable biomass resources, is an ideal carbon sources for the synthesis of functional CDs. PEI is a hydrophilic polymer with a long molecular chain segment and high reaction activity. Its main chain and branch chain have many active amino groups, which was conducive to the surface passivation of xylan-based CDs or the realization of a large number of nitrogen atoms doping in the structure of CDs, forming a more uniform fluorescence emission center. Herein, fluorescent CDs were prepared by hydrothermal synthesis using xylan, polyethyleneimine and urea as starting materials, as shown in Fig. 2A. The morphology and particle size of U-CDs were firstly observed by TEM. As shown in Fig. 2B, U-CDs were monodispersed spherical particles at room temperature. When concentration of CDs reached at 0.5 mg/L, there was no agglomeration. The particle size statistical analysis of approximately 100 monodisperse U-CDs in TEM images was performed using Image J software. The results showed that the majority of U-CDs had particle sizes ranging from 3.0 to 6.0 nm (Fig. 2C), indicating that U-CDs have a wide particle size distribution range. According to the statistical results, the average particle size of N-CDs was approximately 4.5 nm. Fig. 2D showed the HRTEM results, and significant lattice fringes can be observed in U-CDs. According to the selected area diffraction results, the spacing between graphitized carbon core layers was determined to be 0.21 nm, which was consistent with the (100) crystal plane of the graphite phase. The UV visible absorption spectrum in Fig. 2E showed that U-CDs have a maximum absorption wavelength at 350 nm, and the color of U-CDs (QY = 13.9 %) was light brown under a fluorescent lamp. After being irradiated by using UV lamp (365 nm), the color of U-CDs changed to bright blue. The above results indicated that the U-CDs have good fluorescence luminescence properties. Subsequently, the fluorescence emission behavior of U-CDs was studied in Fig. 2F, and the results showed that as the excitation wavelength increased, the emission wavelength of U-CDs gradually changed from excitation independence to excitation dependence. Above results indicated that U-CDs have adjustable excitation characteristics, which can effectively regulate the luminescent properties of xylan-based U-CDs. In the FTIR spectrum (Fig. 2G), the infrared absorption peak located at 3430 cm−1 attributed to stretching vibration of OH. The stretching vibration absorption peak of N − H was located near 3250 cm−1. The characteristic absorption peaks of CH appeared at 2925 cm−1 and 2834 cm−1. The peak appearing near 1629 cm−1 was attributed to the presence of CC or stretching vibration of CN bond. Due to the amination reaction between the intermediate molecules formed by the carbon source during the hydrothermal process and PEI and urea, Obvious CN characteristic absorption peak appears of U-CDs were found at 1460 cm−1. In addition, the absorption band of 1346 cm−1 corresponded to the bending vibration of C-OH, while the infrared absorption peak appearing at 1115 cm−1 and 1043 cm−1 was related to the stretching vibration of C-O-C and CO. The above results showed that the surface of U-CDs contains a large amount of amide groups, which indicates that PEI and urea as nitrogen dopant can effectively realize the nitrogen heterozygosity of xylan-based CDs.

The surface chemical composition of U-CDs was analyzed by using XPS. The results were shown in Fig. 2H–J. The XPS element wide scan results indicated that the signal peaks at the XPS Binding energy of 284.8 eV, 399.5 eV and 532.0 eV were attributed to C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively, indicating that U-CDs were mainly composed of C, O and N elements, respectively. Fig. 2H showed high-resolution XPS spectrum of C1s. Analysis results showed that main existing component forms of C element in U-CDs were C-C/C=C (284.5 eV), C–N (285.1 eV), C–O (285.7 eV), and CO (287.8 eV). The CC bond reflected the presence of a large number of sp2 hybrid carbon atoms in U-CDs structural composition. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of N1s showed XPS signal peaks with binding energies of 398.7ev and 399.5 eV (Fig. 2I), which were related to pyridine N and pyrrole N, respectively. The C1s high-resolution XPS spectrum of U-CDs showed XPS signal peaks with binding energies of 531.1ev, 532.4ev, and 533.5 eV (Fig. 2J), corresponding to CO, C-OH/C-O-C, and CO bonds, respectively.

Owning the U-CDs solution suffers the drawbacks with difficult to store and cannot be reused in the detection process, we use the in-situ electrospinning method to mix the U-CDs and precursor solution uniformly to prepare a solid-state fluorescent nanofiber membrane sensor (NBU-CDs) to detect Cu2+, as shown in Fig. 3A. The microstructure and size distribution of NBU-CDs were characterized using SEM analysis. As shown in Fig. 3B, the fiber distribution of NBU-CDs was uniform and the particle size was uniform, with an average size of 530 nm. And elemental analysis showed that the fiber membrane sensor mainly contains three elements C, N and O, which proving the NBU-CDs has been successful preparation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence property of NBU-CDs for detection Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para: Then we used fluorescence con-focal microscopy to characterize and analyze the micro morphology and fluorescence intensity of fluorescent fiber membrane sensor NBU-CDs. As shown in Fig. 4 , NBU-CDs (QY = 23.3 %) displayed bright green fluorescence signal and the fiber distribution of NBU-CDs was uniform. Importantly, when NBU-CDs combined with Cu2+, the green fluorescence signal exhibited a significant quenching phenomenon. The 3D view also provided a more intuitive display of changes in fluorescence intensity. Above results proved that NBU-CDs has high sensitively for Cu2+.

Then, fluorescence emission spectroscopy was used to quantitatively analyzed the ability of NBU-CDs for detection Cu2+. The results showed that when NBU-CDs was cultured with different metal ions, other metal ions did not have a significant impact on the fluorescence intensity of NBU-CDs. However, when Cu2+ was combined with NBU-CDs, the fluorescence signal of NBU-CDs showed significant quenching. In addition, other metal ions did not have a certain degree of influence on the detection of Cu2+. The above results indicated that NBU-CDs has high sensitivity and selectivity for Cu2+ detection, as shown in Fig. 5A. Next, we speculated on the mechanism of NBU-CDs for Cu2+, as shown in Fig. 5B. A large number of amino groups contained in NBU-CDs could capture Cu2+ and coordinate to form NBU-CDs + Cu2+ complexes. The electron transferred from NBU-CDs to Cu2+ caused the fluorescence signal produce obvious quenching phenomenon. The coordination between the surface groups of NBU-CDs and metal ions is the basis for photo-induced electron transfer (PET). According to reported literature, luminescent materials, synthesized from PEI precursors, can respond to Cu2+ by forming copper ammonia complex, leading to fluorescence quenching. Besides, as we all known, owning the coordination energies of N-containing groups and Cu2+ are in a lower order-NH2 > -NR2 > -CONH- > H2O. There were abundant NH2 groups on the surface of NBU-CDs, which making Cu2+ preferentially coordinate with the surface of the-NH2 of NBU-CDs, increasing the splitting energy of the d orbital. And the amount of Cu2+ on the surface of NBU-CDs was increased, resulting in fluorescence quenching phenomenon. Therefore, compared with other metals, NBU-CDs has a positive sensitively effect on Cu2+. However, there is no selectivity for metal ions of similar valence states, which cannot be explained at present [51]. Furthermore, the sensitivity of NBU-CDs for detection Cu2+ was studied. NBU-CDs showed maximum fluorescence intensity at 410 nm, and with different concentrations Cu2+ was added to the NBU-CDs the fluorescence intensity value at 415 nm gradually appeared quenching and accompanied with red shift, as shown in Fig. 5C. The above results further proved that the addition of Cu2+ led the whole fluorescence system appeared electron transfer, which further leaded to the quenching of fluorescence intensity. In addition, the fluorescence intensity ratio of F 488 nm/F 415 nm and Cu2+ concentration ranged from 0.5 μM to 5 μM exhibited a good linear relationship with F 488nm/F 415nm = 17.944–3.231[Cu2+], R2 = 0.9847. The detection limit of Cu2+ was calculated as 0.83 μM by using formula of 3σ/slope. This detection limit was much lower than the maximum limit for Cu2+ in drinking water set by the United States Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) (1.3 mg/L, ∼20 μM) and the standard limit for domestic water applications in China (1.0 mg/L). Besides, the detection limit value of NBU-CDs was lower than the reported Cu2+ fluorescence sensors, indicating the good sensitively of NBU-CDs for Cu2+ (Table 1 ). Further, in order to further evaluate practical application capability of NBU-CDs as fluorescent sensor, time stability and fluorescence stability were another important parameter. As shown in Fig. 5D, as detection time increased (0–10 days), the fluorescence intensity of NBU-CDs also remained basically stable, proving that NBU-CDs has good fluorescence and time stability and can be used for a long time. Importantly, besides, we also conducted another fluorescence confocal imaging experiment (August 13, 2023) on the NBU-CDs, and the results in Fig. S1 showed that compared with the confocal imaging images taken 479 days ago (April 21, 2022), NBU-CDs still shows bright green fluorescence signals, and the results directly proved that NBU-CDs had better fluorescence stability. And the fluorescence images of U-CDs after 479 days in Fig. S2 also showing that U-CDs have good time and fluorescence stability.

Much research has shown that ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt (EDTA) is an essential complexing agent, it can chelate with transition metal ions and heavy metal ions form water-soluble complexes [57,58]. Recently, it has been widely used in the industrial field owning its excellent chelating properties. Hence, reproducibility ability of NBU-CDs for Cu2+ was investigated by using EDTA via fluorescence emission spectroscopy according to references. As depicted in Fig. S3a–b, with the continuous addition of EDTA (20 μM) to NBU-CDs + Cu2+ mixed system, the fluorescence intensity recovered to a state like that of the free NBU-CDs, which proving that EDTA has good chelating ability to Cu2+. Above results can be attributed that EDTA has excellent chelation ability, and the complex NBU-CDs + Cu2+ can be further decomposed and Cu2+ was released, which attenuated the PET efficiency during the reaction, ultimately leading to fluorescence recovery. On the premise that NBU-CDs detects Cu2+ with a fluorescent signal, cyclic response of NBU-CDs towards Cu2+ and EDTA as the molecular switch was represented by suitable logic gate in Fig. S3c. The fluorescence sensor NBU-CDs and Cu2+ or EDTA as inputs could be marked as ‘1’ and ‘0’ in their presence and absence. The ‘on’ and ‘off’ states of fluorescence signals for outputs were labeled as ‘1’ and ‘0’, respectively. NBU-CDs depicted a strong fluorescence signal (1 state, output). With free Cu2+ as chemical input, fluorescence signal of NBU-CDs quenched (0 state, output). However, while subsequent input of EDTA in this quenched state again regenerated a considerable, fluorescence enhancement signal was produced of NBU-CDs + Cu2+ and the fluorescence signal renewed on (1 state, output). Those behaviors of the switch conversion were also epitomized by suitable logic gates and truth table, as shown in Fig. S3d–e. This sensing system based on the ‘on–off–on’ fluorescence switching method can facilitate further development in Cu2+ detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Practical application of NBU-CDs for detection Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the NBU-CDs' ability for detection Cu2+ in actual environments, we selected soil samples from different locations on campus (pond soil, grassland soil and bamboo soil) and pretreated them. Herein, NBU-CDs were used to qualitatively and quantitatively detect exogenous Cu2+ in various soil samples, respectively, as shown in Fig. 6A. From Fig. 6B–C, it can be seen that under the radiation of ultraviolet light lamp (365 nm), when NBU-CDs was mixed with the soil filter solution, NBU-CDs showed obvious bright blue fluorescence. Then, when exogenous Cu2+ (50 μM) were combined with NBU-CDs, the fluorescence intensity of NBU-CDs showed a significant quenching, which proved that the NBU-CDs has the ability to detect Cu2+ with quantitative and qualitative analysis in the actual environment.

Further, in order to verify the ability of NBU-CDs for Cu2+ detection in the actual water environment. Environment water including school water, Xuanwu Lake water and Yangtze River were used as detection samples, respectively. The concentrations of Cu2+ in various water samples were detected by using labeling recovery method according to the fluorescence titration curve. As shown in Table 2 , the recovery rates of Cu2+ in different water samples were obtained within the range of 97 %–104 %. These results revealed that NBU-CDs can be used as a reliable tool to quantitatively detect Cu2+ with high sensitivity, high precision accuracy and good reliability.

Finally, we validated the bio-compatibility of U-CDs and NBU-CDs, and further investigated the ability of NBU-CDs as fluorescent sensors for detecting Cu2+ in living system. From Fig. 7A and B, it can be seen that when mung beans were co cultured with U-CDs solutions of different concentrations (10 μM, 50 μM), mung beans exhibited significant rooting and germination phenomenon, and with a germination rate greater than 90 %. With the increase of cultivation days (0–8 days), mung beans showed obvious cotyledons. The above results indicate that U-CDs have no toxic or side effects on plant growth within a certain concentration range. Furthermore, we studied the ability of NBU-CDs as fluorescence sensor to detect exogenous Cu2+ in living animals. Results in Fig. 7C and D showed that NBU-CDs had obvious luminescence effect in zebrafish embryos and in vivo, indicating that NBU-CDs could successfully stain living tissues. In addition, when exogenous Cu2+ were added to zebrafish, the fluorescence of zebrafish embryos and adults appeared obviously quenched under fluorescence microscope. These results were consistent with the fluorescence spectrum results, demonstrating the potential of NBU-CDs as fluorescence sensors for detecting exogenous Cu2+. Further, we also verified the biocompatibility of NBU-CDs, and the results showed that the zebrafish still survived after 15 days for co-cultured with NBU-CDs, which further proved that the NBU-CDs had no biological toxicity.

In summary, we have developed a new solid state biomass fluorescence nanofiber membrane (NBU-CDs) used as fluorescence sensor for imaging and biosensing of Cu2+ by composited xylan derived CDs (U-CDs) with PCL/PVA. The developed NBU-CDs used as fluorescence sensor depicted remarkable selectivity and sensitively, low plant cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility. Besides, the high sensitivity and rapid response time of NBU-CDs towered Cu2+ make it possible for tracking the dynamic changes of Cu2+ with detection limit of 0.83 μM. Taking advantages of NBU-CDs, including good selectivity and high accuracy, the developed sensor has successfully applied for Cu2+ sensing and imaging in water and soil environment samples with quantitative and qualitative analysis methods. This study proved the excellent selectivity and stability of the solid-state fluorescent sensing platform, which has promising potential for rapid monitoring Cu2+ in practical application. It should be pointed out that development of biomass nanofiber membrane used as fluorescence sensor with large stokes shift is still challenging. The related research is ongoing.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Yingying Zhang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing original draft.
Ranhua Xiong: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.
Chaobo Huang: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We also thank the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22275094, 22275093) and Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province (KYCX23_1159, SJCX23_0328) for giving us supporting.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2023.126431.
